title: Anxiety in Women: The Under-Discussed Shape
subtitle: A short reading on how anxiety often presents in women and why it gets missed
excerpt: A short reading on how anxiety frequently presents in women (irritability, physical symptoms, high-functioning masking) rather than the clinical picture most descriptions default to, and what to look for in yourself.
sourceNotes: Original to homemade.education. Draws on gender and mental health research and ADAA data on gender differences in anxiety presentation.

practice.whenToUse: Use when you are not sure whether what you experience is anxiety, or when anxiety treatment advice has consistently not matched your experience.

content.text: The standard description of anxiety (racing heart, visible distress, difficulty functioning) describes one presentation. It is not the most common presentation in women. The version that goes unrecognised and untreated for years is quieter and harder to name.
Where the clinical picture came from

The standard picture of anxiety was largely built from studies of men. The visible, physical presentation (hyperventilation, chest tightness, overt distress) was what got measured. Women present with anxiety at twice the rate of men, but the presentation often looks different, and those differences were not the main reference point.

In women, anxiety often shows up as irritability rather than visible fear. As constant checking and perfectionism rather than avoidance. As physical symptoms (fatigue, gut problems, headaches, tension) that get sent to the right doctor before anyone considers anxiety as the cause. As high-functioning masking: continuing to perform while the internal state is one of constant high alert.

The high-functioning presentation is particularly likely to be missed. Someone who continues to meet their commitments, manage other people, and appear capable does not match the distress picture that triggers a referral. The anxiety runs underneath, and the cost accumulates.

Standard advice for anxiety covers slow breathing, facing what you avoid, and questioning worried thoughts. These help with the body and mind side. They are less likely to address what keeps it going for many women: the weight of caregiving, tracking everything for everyone, and the risk of being seen as difficult when it comes out as anger.
What to look for in yourself

Anxiety that does not look anxious. A low-level tension that runs all the time. Trouble switching off even when nothing is wrong. Anger that seems bigger than its cause. Physical symptoms with no clear cause after tests. A constant watching for what might go wrong. These are worth naming as anxiety, even if they do not match the standard picture.
Original to homemade.education. It draws on research into how anxiety shows up in women and why it often goes unrecognised.
